core_concept: The Thirteen Colonies: Life Before the Revolution
facts: The thirteen British colonies along the Atlantic coast were divided into three regions—New England, Middle, and Southern—not just by location but because each region had a different climate and geography that shaped its economy and society.
New England colonies had rocky soil and short growing seasons, so colonists there relied more on fishing, shipbuilding, and trade than on large-scale farming, unlike the Southern colonies.
Southern colonies had warm weather and fertile soil, which allowed large plantations to grow cash crops like tobacco and rice, but this system depended on enslaved labor.
The Middle Atlantic colonies, with their deep rivers and rich farmland, became known as the 'breadbasket' because they grew large amounts of wheat and other grains for export.

Even though all thirteen colonies were part of the British Empire, their societies varied: for example, New England towns were often centered around a meetinghouse, while Southern plantations were spread far apart.
misconceptions: Students may think all thirteen colonies had the same economy, believing that every colony grew tobacco, but only the Southern colonies had the warm climate and soil needed for that cash crop.

Students may believe the Middle colonies had the same rocky soil and cold winters as New England, not realizing the Middle region had fertile land and milder weather that made it ideal for grain farming.
